before_code:
⟨_, (multipliableLocallyUniformlyOn_one_sub_pow.hasProdLocallyUniformlyOn :
    TendstoLocallyUniformlyOn _ _ _ _).comp (Periodic.qParam 1)
  (fun z hz ↦ by simpa using ...) (by fun_prop)⟩
after_code:
-- after adding a general `MultipliableLocallyUniformlyOn.comp` lemma upstream:
multipliableLocallyUniformlyOn_one_sub_pow.comp (𝕢 1)
  (fun z hz ↦ by simpa using ...) (by fun_prop)
description: During review/cleanup, actively look for places where a general lemma is missing — not just style/golf issues. The signal is a proof that uses an ad-hoc workaround (e.g., ⟨_, (h.something : SomeOtherType _ _ _).comp _ ...⟩ instead of h.comp ...). The way to find these: read the proof and ask 'what would make this nicer / shorter / more conceptual?'. If the answer is 'if there were a .comp lemma for this namespace', the right fix is to add the general lemma upstream, not to keep the workaround. The MultipliableLocallyUniformlyOn.comp case is the canonical example: a missing dot-notation lemma forced ⟨_, ...⟩ and a type ascription.

before_code: simp [show ¬ Even 1 from by decide] at hk2
after_code: simp [show ¬ Even 1 by decide] at hk2
description: Don't write `show X from by Y` — the `from` is redundant. Use `show X by Y` directly. The `from` keyword is for ascribing a *term*: `show X from term` says the term has type `X`. When the term is itself `by tactic`, the `from` adds nothing — `show X by tactic` already means 'prove the goal X using tactic'.
